items.title: Pune ITAT: Sanction for Reassessment Taken from Wrong Authority Renders Section 148 Notice Void
items.summary: Reassessment proceedings collapse when approval under Section 151 is obtained from PCIT instead of the prescribed PCCIT — Tribunal holds that jurisdictional error cannot be condoned at any stage.
items.body:
Background

The assessee, an individual taxpayer based in Pune, received a notice under Section 148 of the Income-tax Act for Assessment Year 2018-19. The Assessing Officer had obtained prior sanction from the Principal Commissioner of Income Tax (PCIT) before issuing the reassessment notice. The assessee challenged the notice on the ground that the required approval should have been obtained from the Principal Chief Commissioner of Income Tax (PCCIT), who is the designated 'specified authority' for cases involving income exceeding Rs 50 lakh where the original assessment period had elapsed beyond three years.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The core question was whether sanction obtained from PCIT — instead of the PCCIT as mandated under the proviso to Section 151(ii) — is a curable irregularity or a fatal jurisdictional defect that voids the entire reassessment proceeding.

Decision

The Pune Bench of the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal held that the requirement to obtain approval from the correct specified authority under Section 151 is not a mere procedural formality — it is a substantive safeguard built into the reassessment framework to prevent arbitrary exercise of power by the Revenue. The Tribunal observed that Section 151 specifically prescribes which authority must grant sanction depending on the nature and age of the case. Where the prescribed authority has not granted sanction, the notice under Section 148 is issued without valid jurisdiction and must be treated as non-est in law.

The Tribunal further noted that the Supreme Court in Union of India v. Ashish Agarwal and subsequent decisions of various High Courts have consistently held that compliance with Section 151 is mandatory, not directory. Approval by a lower-ranked authority does not constitute substantial compliance — it is a complete absence of the required sanction in the eyes of law.

Significance

This ruling reinforces a growing line of judicial authority that protects taxpayers against mechanically issued reassessment notices. Tax practitioners should immediately examine whether, in all pending reassessment proceedings beyond the three-year period, approval was obtained from the correct tier of authority. Where PCIT approval was obtained for cases requiring PCCIT sanction, a challenge on jurisdictional grounds stands on strong legal footing.

items.title: ITAT Mumbai Deletes Rs 64 Lakh On-Money Addition: Excel Sheet Entries Without Corroboration Cannot Ground a Section 69 Addition
items.summary: Tribunal holds that unverified spreadsheet data and unilateral third-party statements — without supporting physical evidence or cross-examination — are wholly insufficient to sustain an addition for unexplained investments.
items.body:
Background

During survey and search proceedings, the Revenue seized an Excel spreadsheet from the premises of a property broker that allegedly recorded on-money receipts in real estate transactions involving the Rubberwala Group. Based on entries in this spreadsheet and statements recorded from the broker's associates, the Assessing Officer added Rs 64 lakh to the assessee's income under Section 69 as unexplained investment, treating the alleged on-money as unaccounted cash paid over and above the registered sale consideration.

Assessee's Defence

The assessee contended that the Excel sheet was a unilateral document prepared by a third party without any participation by the assessee and that the entries were not corroborated by any independent evidence such as cash flow records, bank statements showing unexplained withdrawals, or direct witness testimony subject to cross-examination. The assessee also pointed out that the registered sale deeds reflected the actual consideration paid and that all transactions had been accounted for in the books.

Decision

The Mumbai Bench of the ITAT set aside the addition in its entirety. The Tribunal held that additions under Section 69 must rest on concrete, cogent and corroborated evidence. A spreadsheet maintained by a third party — which the assessee had no opportunity to contest or cross-examine — cannot by itself constitute reliable evidence of unexplained investment. The Tribunal observed that the Revenue's entire case was built on the 'Excel sheet theory' without a single piece of independent corroboration: no cash trail, no admission by the assessee, and no independent witness whose testimony was subject to testing in adversarial proceedings.

The Tribunal further held that natural justice demands that where additions are based on third-party statements, the assessee must be given a full opportunity to cross-examine those witnesses. Denial of cross-examination, even in addition to the quality of evidence being poor, independently renders the addition unsustainable.

Key Takeaway

This decision is significant for taxpayers facing on-money addition cases in real estate transactions. It lays down that the Revenue must go beyond seized documents and establish each element of the alleged cash transaction through independent, admissible and cross-examined evidence. The burden of proof in Section 69 cases remains on the Revenue to establish that the investment was made and that it remains unexplained.

items.title: Section 76 CGST: Why Genuine Buyers Cannot Be Punished for the Supplier's Default in Depositing Tax
items.summary: A detailed analysis of the statutory protection available to bona fide purchasers under Section 76 of the CGST Act — and why denial of ITC to genuine buyers whose suppliers have collected but not deposited tax is constitutionally and legally untenable.
items.body:
The Problem

One of the most contentious battlegrounds under the GST regime in recent years has been the denial of Input Tax Credit (ITC) to buyers whose suppliers have collected GST from them but failed to deposit it with the government. Tax authorities have frequently sought to deny ITC to the purchasing entity under Rule 36(4) read with Section 16 of the CGST Act, placing the entire burden of the supplier's default on the innocent buyer who has already paid the tax as part of the invoice consideration.

Section 76: The Legislative Answer

Section 76 of the CGST Act provides a complete and independent mechanism for recovery of tax collected but not deposited. The provision empowers Revenue to pursue the defaulting supplier directly for the amounts collected. The legislative intent, as reflected in the statement of objects and reasons for the CGST Act, is clear: the registered supplier who collects tax from the buyer is a collecting agent of the government. If the agent misappropriates the collection, the government's remedy lies against the agent — not the innocent party who paid in good faith.

Constitutional Dimensions

Multiple High Courts, including Gujarat, Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh, have now struck down or read down provisions that result in double taxation of the buyer where the supplier has demonstrably collected and failed to remit the tax. The Gujarat High Court in particular held that denying ITC where the tax has in fact been paid by the buyer — merely because the supplier failed in its fiscal duty — amounts to an arbitrary exercise of state power violating Article 14 of the Constitution.

Practical Guidance for Taxpayers

Businesses facing ITC denial should document: (a) the invoice and payment records showing actual disbursement of GST consideration to the supplier; (b) any prior GST return filings of the supplier showing the transactions; and (c) evidence that reasonable due diligence was exercised before transacting with the supplier. Armed with this documentation, a challenge before the appropriate authority under Section 76 and a writ before the High Court presents a strong case for protecting legitimately accrued ITC.

items.title: Delhi HC to Revenue: 100% Shareholding in a Foreign Company Does Not Make You the Owner of Its Assets
items.summary: High Court reaffirms the fundamental principle of corporate separateness — income of a company cannot be attributed to its shareholders merely by reason of ownership, however complete, unless fraud or sham is specifically established.
items.body:
Facts

The case involved an Indian resident who held 100% of the share capital of a foreign company incorporated in Mauritius. The foreign company earned rental income from properties located abroad and also realised capital gains on the sale of overseas assets. The Assessing Officer sought to tax both the rental income and capital gains in the hands of the Indian shareholder, reasoning that since the shareholder owned all the shares of the company, the company's income was effectively the shareholder's own income.

Revenue's Argument

The Revenue argued that the corporate structure was a façade and that the foreign company was merely a vehicle through which the Indian resident held and managed the overseas assets. The AO relied on the concept of 'beneficial ownership' and argued that the shareholder, as the ultimate economic beneficiary, should be taxed on all income flowing through the foreign entity.

Court's Ruling

The Delhi High Court emphatically rejected this approach. The Court held that a company is a distinct legal person under law — a principle established since the House of Lords' landmark ruling in Salomon v. Salomon and accepted as part of Indian corporate jurisprudence without exception. Mere ownership of shares, however complete, does not vest ownership of the company's property in the shareholders. The company's income belongs to the company; the shareholder's entitlement is only to dividends as and when declared.

The Court further held that the Revenue's reliance on 'beneficial ownership' was misplaced in this context. The concept of beneficial ownership under tax treaties addresses attribution between contracting states for purposes of withholding tax — it does not dissolve the corporate veil in the manner sought by the AO. To pierce the corporate veil, the Revenue must specifically plead and prove fraud, sham or that the entity was incorporated with the sole purpose of tax evasion. No such finding had been recorded.

Implications

This judgment is particularly relevant for Indian resident individuals and families holding overseas wealth through foreign holding companies. It confirms that cross-border structures maintained in good faith with genuine commercial purpose are entitled to the full protection of corporate law. The Revenue cannot shortcut its way to taxation by ignoring corporate form without establishing the necessary factual foundation for lifting the veil.

items.title: ITAT Kolkata: Late Filing of Form 67 Is a Procedural Lapse — Foreign Tax Credit Cannot Be Denied on This Ground Alone
items.summary: Tribunal holds that Section 90 read with Rule 128 does not create a substantive condition that extinguishes the right to Foreign Tax Credit merely because Form 67 was filed after the return due date.
items.body:
Background

The assessee, an Indian resident employed by a US-based company, had taxes withheld on salary income earned in the United States. On return to India, the assessee claimed Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) under Section 90 of the Income-tax Act read with the India-USA Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement. However, Form 67 — the prescribed form for claiming FTC — was filed after the due date of the income tax return under Section 139(1).

The Assessing Officer denied the FTC claim entirely on the ground that Rule 128(9) of the Income-tax Rules requires Form 67 to be filed on or before the due date of furnishing the return of income. Since the form was filed late, the AO held that the assessee had forfeited the right to credit.

Tribunal's Analysis

The ITAT Kolkata examined whether the requirement of filing Form 67 by the return due date is a mandatory condition (non-compliance with which extinguishes the right) or a directory condition (non-compliance with which may attract penalty but cannot destroy the substantive right).

The Tribunal held that the right to Foreign Tax Credit flows from Section 90 of the Act and the underlying DTAA — both of which are substantive provisions of law. A procedural rule prescribing the format and timing for a claim cannot override or extinguish a substantive right conferred by a statute or treaty. The Tribunal relied on the principle that 'directory' provisions are those where the legislature's intent is to facilitate compliance and not to create traps for taxpayers who make honest late submissions without causing prejudice to Revenue.

Decision

The Tribunal directed the Assessing Officer to allow the FTC claim after verifying the underlying documents establishing the tax paid in the USA. The delay in filing Form 67, without any finding of mala fide intent, was held to be a curable procedural defect — not a ground for permanent forfeiture of a treaty-based credit.

Significance

Expatriates and globally mobile Indian taxpayers frequently face practical difficulties in collating foreign tax certificates and filing Form 67 within the ITR due date. This decision provides important relief and is consistent with a larger body of jurisprudence holding that FTC is a treaty right that cannot be defeated by procedural timelines in domestic rules.

items.title: Karnataka HC: A Single GST Show Cause Notice Can Validly Cover Multiple Financial Years
items.summary: High Court settles a procedural dispute — consolidated SCNs covering more than one tax period are legally maintainable under Sections 73 and 74 of the CGST Act, and the department is not required to issue separate notices for each year.
items.body:
Background

A Karnataka-based registered taxpayer received a consolidated show cause notice under Section 73 of the CGST Act covering alleged tax shortfalls across three consecutive financial years — 2018-19, 2019-20 and 2020-21. The taxpayer challenged the notice on the ground that the law contemplated one notice per tax period and that a consolidated notice covering multiple years was procedurally incompetent.

Court's Ruling

The Karnataka High Court dismissed the writ petition and upheld the validity of consolidated SCNs under the CGST Act. The Court held that neither Section 73 nor Section 74 expressly restricts the issuance of a show cause notice to a single financial year. The law prescribes what must be stated in a notice and the timelines within which it must be issued — it does not mandate fragmentation of proceedings where the same taxpayer faces similar allegations spanning multiple periods.

The Court reasoned that consolidation, if anything, serves the interests of efficiency and reduces the procedural burden on taxpayers who would otherwise have to respond to multiple parallel proceedings. The only requirement is that the notice must clearly specify the amount alleged to be due for each tax period so that the taxpayer is not prejudiced in framing its reply.

Practical Implications

This ruling is important for businesses facing multi-year GST audit proceedings. It confirms that a notice covering, say, FY 2019-20 to FY 2022-23 in one document is not inherently bad in law. Taxpayers should, however, insist on period-wise breakdowns in the SCN and in any order that may follow, so that the quantum of demand, interest and penalty for each year is determinable and challengeable independently.

The ruling also has implications for limitation — taxpayers must verify that the SCN was issued within the time limits prescribed under Section 73(10) or Section 74(10) for each of the years covered by the notice. A consolidated SCN does not extend the limitation applicable to each individual tax period.

items.title: Delhi HC: Additional Documents Can Be Produced in Tax Prosecution — Courts Must Seek Truth, Not Defeat It on Technicality
items.summary: High Court allows production of supplementary documents under Section 311 CrPC in a criminal income tax prosecution, holding that procedural rigidity must yield where substantive justice and truth-finding require it.
items.body:
Background

Criminal prosecution under Chapter XXII of the Income-tax Act was initiated against a taxpayer for wilful evasion of tax. During the trial, the prosecution sought to produce additional documents that had been referenced in the complaint but had not been formally exhibited when the case was originally filed. The accused objected to the production of these documents at a later stage, contending that allowing them would prejudice the defence and violated established criminal procedure.

Court's Analysis

The Delhi High Court examined the scope of Section 311 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, which empowers a court to summon any person as a witness or require the production of any document at any stage of a proceeding if it is essential for a just decision. The Court held that this provision is expansive by design — it reflects the cardinal principle that courts are instruments of justice and their primary obligation is to arrive at the truth, not to become prisoners of procedural formalism.

The Court noted that the documents in question had already been listed in the complaint itself — they were not new material being introduced to improve a weak case after the evidence stage. Their production at a slightly later stage therefore did not amount to a fresh case being sprung on the accused. The Court further observed that any potential prejudice to the defence could be adequately addressed by permitting the accused sufficient time to examine and respond to the documents.

The Broader Principle

The Court's ruling contains a notable observation: 'Truth cannot be sacrificed at the altar of procedural technicalities where the resulting injustice would be manifest and where the defect can be adequately remedied short of exclusion.' This principle has wide application in tax prosecution cases where the Revenue frequently faces procedural objections designed to exclude probative material.

What This Means for Tax Litigation

Both taxpayers and the Revenue should take note that criminal tax proceedings are not insulated from the general rules of evidence flexibility that courts exercise in the interest of justice. However, this ruling also cautions that courts will closely scrutinise whether production of documents at a late stage genuinely prejudices the accused — and will not routinely allow procedural gaps to be plugged where the evidence is truly new or the defence is materially prejudiced.

items.title: ITAT Bangalore: Genuine Business Sales During Demonetisation Cannot Be Treated as Unexplained Cash Credits Under Section 68
items.summary: Tribunal deletes Rs 29.27 lakh addition — holds that accepting Specified Bank Notes for actual business transactions is not itself evidence of unexplained income merely because SBNs were banned tender at the time.
items.body:
Background

The assessee ran a retail business and received cash payments from customers during the demonetisation period (November 8–December 30, 2016) in Specified Bank Notes (SBNs — the old Rs 500 and Rs 1,000 currency notes that ceased to be legal tender). These receipts were reflected in the audited accounts as sales income. The Assessing Officer, however, treated the aggregate SBN deposits during this period as unexplained cash credits under Section 68 and added Rs 29.27 lakh to the assessee's income.

Revenue's Position

The AO argued that since SBNs were being deposited with the specific intent of laundering unaccounted cash, the mere fact of receiving SBNs from customers during this period raised a presumption of unexplained income. The assessee had not identified each customer who paid in SBNs, which the AO treated as a failure to discharge the onus under Section 68.

Tribunal's Ruling

The ITAT Bangalore rejected the Revenue's reasoning comprehensively. The Tribunal held that Section 68 applies where the nature and source of a credit entry in the books is 'not satisfactorily explained' — it does not apply where the assessee has fully accounted for the receipts as business income and reflected them in audited financial statements. The nature of the receipts (business sales) and the source (customers purchasing goods) were both clear from the books.

The Tribunal further held that the fact that customers chose to pay in SBNs during the demonetisation window — which was legally permissible for specified transactions under the government's own notifications — does not, by itself, taint the underlying transaction. The business made genuine sales, received genuine payment, and disclosed everything in its accounts. The Revenue could not convert a policy decision about currency withdrawal into a tool for presumptive taxation without specific evidence of actual evasion.

Significance

This ruling is part of a growing body of ITAT decisions clarifying that demonetisation-era additions under Section 68 require the same quality of evidence as any other unexplained credit case. The mere receipt of SBNs, without specific evidence connecting the deposit to unaccounted income, is not sufficient to sustain an addition. Retailers, traders and service providers with audited books who reflected SBN deposits as business income are well-placed to challenge such additions.

items.title: Bombay HC: Reassessment Order Passed After Limitation Period Is Void — Remand Does Not Extend the Clock
items.summary: High Court quashes reassessment for AY concerned after finding that the time limit under Section 153 expired before the AO passed the final order — procedural remand directions by appellate authorities do not revive or extend the limitation.
items.body:
Background

Reassessment proceedings were initiated against the assessee for a particular assessment year. The matter travelled through appeals — the CIT(A) set aside the assessment and remanded it to the AO for fresh adjudication. The AO thereafter passed a fresh reassessment order, but by the time this order was made, the time limit prescribed under Section 153 of the Income-tax Act for completion of reassessment proceedings had already expired.

Revenue's Argument

The Revenue contended that a remand order by the first appellate authority effectively extends the time available to the AO because the AO is required to give effect to the appellate direction. It was argued that where an authority directs fresh consideration, limitation cannot be used as a shield to prevent the Revenue from completing the directed task.

Court's Ruling

The Bombay High Court held that limitation under Section 153 is an absolute bar — once the prescribed period expires, the AO is divested of jurisdiction to pass or complete a reassessment order. The Court examined Section 153(6) which provides for extended time in remand situations and held that its applicability is limited and conditional: it does not automatically apply to all remands and certainly does not create open-ended extension that the Revenue can invoke at any time.

The Court observed that if the Revenue's argument were accepted, it would effectively render the time limits in Section 153 meaningless — any taxpayer who secured a remand at the appellate stage would permanently lose the protection of limitation. That cannot be the legislative intent.

Importance

This decision is highly significant for taxpayers whose cases have been remanded multiple times. A detailed calendar analysis should be undertaken to determine whether any pending reassessment order — if passed pursuant to a remand — may be beyond the statutory limitation. Where limitation has expired, the order is a nullity that can be challenged by way of writ without going through the appellate hierarchy.

items.title: ITAT Delhi: Undisclosed Income Declared Under IDS 2016 But Not Paid Within Time Must Be Taxed in Year of Declaration, Not Original Year
items.summary: Tribunal holds that the Income Declaration Scheme, 2016 creates its own charging mechanism — where conditions are not fulfilled, income crystallises in the year of declaration and cannot be added back to the original year of alleged evasion.
items.body:
Background

The assessee made a declaration under the Income Declaration Scheme, 2016 (IDS) in respect of undisclosed income for Assessment Year 2013-14. The IDS required declarants to pay tax, surcharge and penalty in instalments by specified dates. The assessee declared the income but failed to pay the full amount within the prescribed timelines. The Revenue thereafter initiated reassessment for AY 2013-14 and added the declared amount to the income for that year as though it were regular undisclosed income coming to light during assessment.

Issue

The question was: where an assessee declares income under IDS but fails to complete the payment obligation, in which year should the declared income be taxed — the original year to which the undisclosed income related (AY 2013-14), or the year in which the declaration was made (AY 2016-17)?

Decision

The ITAT Delhi held that reassessment of the declared income in AY 2013-14 was legally incorrect. The Tribunal reasoned that the Income Declaration Scheme, 2016 is a self-contained code. Section 193 of the Finance Act, 2016 (which enacted the IDS) provides that failure to pay the full tax under the scheme results in the declaration being treated as void and the declarant being chargeable to tax under the provisions of the Income-tax Act. However, the tax is to be computed on the income declared in the year corresponding to the declaration — not the original year of evasion.

The Tribunal noted that the entire rationale of the IDS was to encourage voluntary disclosure. Reading the provision to allow the Revenue to go back to the original year of evasion and reopen completed assessments would defeat the scheme's purpose and expose declarants to additional interest and penalty exposure beyond what the scheme contemplated.

Practical Takeaway

This decision is relevant for assessees who made IDS declarations but defaulted on payment. The addition, if any, must be made in the year of declaration — not in the historical year of the alleged undisclosed income. Any reassessment for the original year based solely on the IDS declaration should be challenged on this ground.

items.title: CBDT Notifies Form 10-IEA: Business Taxpayers Who Want Old Regime for AY 2026-27 Must File This Form Before ITR Due Date
items.summary: The Central Board of Direct Taxes has prescribed the new Form 10-IEA for individuals and HUFs carrying on business or profession who wish to opt out of the default new tax regime and continue under the old regime for Assessment Year 2026-27.
items.body:
What Has Been Notified

CBDT has formally notified Form 10-IEA as the prescribed form for exercising the option under Section 115BAC(6) of the Income-tax Act, 2025 (corresponding to the earlier Section 115BAC(6) of the 1961 Act). This form must be filed by individuals and Hindu Undivided Families who carry on a business or profession and wish to opt out of the new default tax regime for Assessment Year 2026-27.

Who Needs to File

Not all taxpayers need to file Form 10-IEA. Only individuals and HUFs with business or professional income fall within its scope. Salaried employees and individuals with only investment income can choose between the old and new regime simply by making the appropriate selection in their ITR — they do not need to file a separate form. However, for business taxpayers, the option to opt out of the new regime is a one-time election that has continuing effect, and Form 10-IEA is the mechanism for that election.

Deadline and Process

The form must be filed electronically on the income tax portal at www.incometax.gov.in on or before the due date for filing the income tax return under Section 139(1) — which for individuals and HUFs not subject to audit is July 31, 2026. Filing after this date will not be accepted by the system, and the taxpayer will automatically be assessed under the new default regime.

Important Note for Continuing Opt-Outs

Taxpayers who had already filed Form 10-IEA or its predecessor for Assessment Year 2025-26 and have maintained the old regime continuously need not re-file Form 10-IEA for AY 2026-27 unless they wish to switch back to the new regime. Once the old regime election is made for business taxpayers, it remains in force until the taxpayer chooses to revert — and reverting can only be done once in a lifetime for business taxpayers.

Compliance Action Point

Tax practitioners should immediately review the status of all business clients to identify those who were in the old regime in AY 2025-26. For those transitioning to business income for the first time in AY 2026-27, Form 10-IEA must be filed proactively. Missing this deadline means mandatory assessment under the new regime regardless of whether the old regime is more beneficial in that year.

items.title: Supreme Court: Pre-Issuance Satisfaction Note Is a Jurisdictional Must Under Section 153C — No Post-Hoc Ratification Permitted
items.summary: A three-judge Bench holds that the AO's satisfaction that seized documents 'belong to' the searched person's associate must be recorded before the Section 153C notice is issued — absence of a prior satisfaction note renders the entire proceedings void ab initio.
items.body:
Background

During the course of a search operation under Section 132 at the premises of a business group, documents and digital records were seized that allegedly contained references to transactions involving an associated entity not directly searched. The documents were forwarded to the Assessing Officer of the associated entity (the 'other person' under Section 153C), who issued a notice under Section 153C and proceeded to make a large addition to the other person's income. The assessee challenged the proceedings on the ground that no satisfaction note had been recorded by the AO before issuing the notice — the satisfaction note was produced only after the addition was made.

The Law on Satisfaction Notes

Section 153C of the Income-tax Act requires the AO of the searched person to forward documents to the AO of the 'other person' after recording satisfaction that the seized material belongs to the other person. The legal question before the Court was whether this satisfaction note is a jurisdictional pre-condition or merely a procedural requirement that can be satisfied at any point during the proceedings.

Supreme Court's Decision

The three-judge Bench held unequivocally that the satisfaction note is a condition precedent — it must be recorded before the notice under Section 153C is issued. The Court observed that the satisfaction note is the very foundation of the AO's jurisdiction over the 'other person': without it, the AO has no power to issue the notice, conduct the assessment or make any addition. A post-hoc note prepared after the assessment is complete amounts to no note at all — it is a retrospective attempt to supply a jurisdictional requirement that cannot be supplied retrospectively.

The Court further clarified that the phrase 'belong to' in Section 153C means that the documents must, in the AO's reasoned opinion, be the documents of the other person — documents that merely mention the other person or are relevant to the other person's transactions do not suffice. The satisfaction must be document-specific and reflect independent application of mind.

Impact

This ruling will have sweeping consequences for pending search assessment cases across the country. Any Section 153C proceeding where the satisfaction note was either absent or was prepared after the notice was issued is now vulnerable to being quashed. Practitioners representing assessees in such matters should immediately call for the satisfaction note and examine its date and content relative to the date of the Section 153C notice.

items.title: 55th GST Council: Prosecution Threshold Raised to Rs 3 Crore; Term Insurance Fully Exempt; Major Rate Rationalisation Approved
items.summary: The 55th GST Council meeting, chaired by the Union Finance Minister, approved significant changes to the GST prosecution framework and rate structure — including full exemption for term life insurance and raising the monetary limit for criminal proceedings under the CGST Act.
items.body:
Prosecution Threshold Raised

In what is being described as the most significant decriminalisation step since the GST regime's launch, the 55th GST Council has recommended raising the monetary threshold for initiating criminal prosecution under the CGST Act from Rs 2 crore to Rs 3 crore. This means that tax evasion cases where the disputed amount is below Rs 3 crore will not automatically attract criminal proceedings — the Revenue will have to rely on adjudication and recovery proceedings under the civil framework.

The Council has also recommended liberalising the compounding provisions, reducing compounding amounts and broadening the class of offences eligible for compounding. This is intended to reduce the chilling effect of criminal prosecution on businesses and encourage voluntary compliance and settlement of disputes.

Term Life Insurance: Full GST Exemption

The Council has recommended that term life insurance premiums — where the policy provides only a death benefit without any maturity or savings component — be fully exempted from GST. Currently, term insurance premiums are subject to 18% GST. This change will directly benefit a large number of individual policyholders and is expected to improve insurance penetration in the country.

The exemption is proposed to apply prospectively from a notified date. Existing policyholders on premium payment dates after the notification will benefit from the reduced cost.

Health Insurance Rationalisation

The Council has also taken up rationalisation of GST on health insurance premiums. While a full exemption for individual health insurance was debated, the Council recommended a reduced rate of 5% on health insurance premiums for individuals — down from the current 18%. Senior citizen health insurance is proposed to be fully exempt.

Other Rate Changes

Several other rate changes were recommended, including rationalisation of rates on specific goods and services that had been subject to inverted duty structure issues. The complete list of rate changes will be given effect through notifications issued by the Ministry of Finance following the Council's recommendations.

items.title: ITAT Mumbai Special Bench: Section 56(2)(x) Does Not Apply to Shares Transferred in NCLT-Approved Group Restructuring
items.summary: Special Bench holds that the fair market value deeming provision under Rule 11UA cannot override the substance and legal sanction of a court-approved restructuring — addition of Rs 18.4 crore deleted.
items.body:
Background

In the course of a group restructuring sanctioned by the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), shares were transferred from one group company to another at a consideration that was less than their fair market value as computed under Rule 11UA of the Income-tax Rules. The Assessing Officer invoked Section 56(2)(x) of the Income-tax Act and added the difference between the FMV and the actual consideration — amounting to Rs 18.4 crore — to the transferee company's income as 'income from other sources.'

The Legal Conflict

Section 56(2)(x) provides that where any person receives any property, including shares, for inadequate consideration, the shortfall from fair market value shall be treated as income in the hands of the recipient. The issue before the Special Bench was whether this provision could apply to a transfer that was sanctioned by the NCLT as part of a duly approved restructuring scheme — where the consideration was fixed by the court and parties had no freedom to negotiate it independently.

Special Bench Decision

The Special Bench of the ITAT Mumbai held in favour of the assessee. The Bench reasoned that Section 56(2)(x) was enacted to tackle tax avoidance through sham or under-valued transactions at arm's length where parties could otherwise negotiate consideration to suit their tax planning. It was not designed to apply to transactions mandated and sanctioned by a court of law, where the consideration is fixed under judicial supervision for the purpose of reorganisation in the public interest.

The Bench further held that the Rule 11UA deeming mechanism — which mechanically computes FMV for tax purposes — cannot be used to override the commercial and legal reality of a NCLT-approved transaction. The scheme sanctioned by NCLT has the force of a court order, and parties to it are bound by it. There is no question of the transferee having 'received' property at inadequate consideration in any voluntary sense that Section 56(2)(x) was meant to address.

Why This Matters

Corporate restructurings through NCLT are common instruments for demergers, mergers and consolidation within business groups. This Special Bench ruling provides important protection against deeming addition in such cases and is likely to be cited extensively in corporate tax matters. The ruling also raises a broader principle: deeming provisions in tax law must be interpreted in light of their anti-avoidance purpose and should not be applied mechanically to transactions that fall outside that purpose.

items.title: ITAT Mumbai: Reassessment Notice Not Routed Through Faceless Mechanism Under Section 151A Is Jurisdictionally Invalid
items.summary: Tribunal holds that once the faceless regime under Section 151A became operative, issuance of a Section 148 notice through the conventional offline route is without jurisdiction — the entire reassessment including a Rs 1.61 crore addition collapses.
items.body:
Background

The assessee received a notice under Section 148 for Assessment Year 2018-19. On examination, it was found that the notice had been issued by the jurisdictional Assessing Officer through the conventional route rather than through the Faceless Assessment Centre as required under Section 151A of the Income-tax Act. By the time the notice was issued, CBDT had notified the Faceless Reassessment scheme, making it mandatory for all reassessment notices under Section 148 to be issued and processed through the faceless mechanism.

The AO proceeded with assessment despite the challenge and made an addition of Rs 1.61 crore under Section 68 treating certain credits in the assessee's books as unexplained. The assessee challenged both the jurisdiction and the addition before the Tribunal.

Tribunal's Ruling on Jurisdiction

The Mumbai Bench of the ITAT held that Section 151A is not a technical provision — it is a structural cornerstone of the faceless assessment architecture introduced to eliminate human interface between taxpayers and tax officials. The mandatory channelling of reassessment proceedings through the designated faceless infrastructure is a jurisdictional requirement. A notice issued outside this framework by an individual AO is issued without authority of law and is void from the outset.

The Tribunal applied the settled legal principle that where a statute prescribes a particular mode for the exercise of a power, that power must be exercised only in that mode — exercise in any other mode is not a mere irregularity but a nullity. Since the foundational notice was void, all subsequent proceedings including the assessment order and the addition under Section 68 could not survive.

Broader Implications

This ruling adds to a body of jurisprudence protecting taxpayers against reassessment proceedings initiated in violation of the faceless framework. Every reassessment notice issued after CBDT operationalised the Faceless Reassessment Scheme should be scrutinised for compliance with Section 151A. Where the conventional route was followed, the notice and all proceedings pursuant to it are open to challenge on jurisdictional grounds, irrespective of the merits of the underlying addition.

items.title: ITAT Chennai: PCIT Cannot Grant Sanction for Reassessment Beyond Three Years — Only 'Specified Authority' Under Section 151(ii) Has That Power
items.summary: Tribunal quashes reassessment for AY 2016-17 where PCIT granted approval — holds that for notices issued after the three-year period, only the designated 'specified authority' (PCCIT/CCIT/PCIT of a defined higher rank) has jurisdiction to sanction.
items.source: ITAT Chennai — Aman Gupta vs DCIT
items.body:
Facts

A reassessment notice was issued for Assessment Year 2016-17 — which is beyond the three-year period from the end of the relevant assessment year. Under the amended Section 151(ii), notices issued after three years can only be sanctioned by the 'specified authority' — which for such cases is the Principal Chief Commissioner or Chief Commissioner of Income Tax. The AO in this case obtained approval from the Principal Commissioner (PCIT), who is a lower-ranked authority than required.

The assessee, Aman Gupta, challenged the notice on jurisdictional grounds. The addition made was under Section 69A for alleged unexplained money in a bank account.

Tribunal's Analysis

The ITAT Chennai examined the amended Section 151 introduced with effect from April 1, 2022. Pre-amendment, the PCIT was a competent authority to grant sanction. Post-amendment, the law distinguishes between cases within three years (where the PCIT/CIT has sanctioning authority) and cases beyond three years (where only the higher-ranked 'specified authority' can sanction). The legislative intent was clearly to add an additional safeguard for older cases where the assessee's reasonable expectation of finality is stronger.

The Tribunal held that the sanction by PCIT for a beyond-three-year case is not a technical defect — it is an absence of jurisdiction at the foundational level. There is no provision for ratification or condoning of this error by any court or authority. The notice is void and the assessment proceedings have no legal legs to stand on.

Key Learning

Practitioners must map every reassessment notice to the applicable limitation bracket and verify that the sanctioning authority belongs to the correct tier. For AYs beyond three years from the end of the assessment year, only the PCCIT or CCIT has sanctioning authority. Any deviation — even if the PCIT who granted sanction is otherwise senior in the department's internal hierarchy — makes the notice void.

items.title: Gauhati HC: GST Show Cause Notice Issued One Day Before Three-Month Deadline Is Valid — Corresponding Date Rule Applies
items.summary: High Court dismisses challenge to an SCN under Section 73 CGST for excess ITC for FY 2020-21 — holds that notice issued on November 29 with an order deadline of February 28 satisfies the three-month requirement by applying the corresponding date rule.
items.source: Gauhati HC — Surya Business Pvt Ltd vs State of Assam
items.body:
Background

Surya Business Private Limited, a registered GST taxpayer in Assam, challenged a show cause notice issued under Section 73(2) of the CGST Act alleging excess Input Tax Credit claimed for FY 2020-21. The notice was issued on November 29, 2024. The order under Section 73 was required to be passed by February 28, 2025 — giving the assessee approximately three months between notice and order as required by Section 73(2).

The taxpayer's primary challenge was that the notice was issued only one day before the three-month deadline expired and that this did not give it adequate time to respond before the order date. The taxpayer sought to have the SCN quashed as being issued in breach of the mandatory three-month notice requirement.

Court's Ruling

The Gauhati High Court dismissed the writ petition. The Court held that Section 73(2) requires a minimum of three months between the date of the show cause notice and the date of the final order. In computing whether this gap was satisfied, the Court applied the 'corresponding date rule' — a well-established principle in limitation and calendar computation drawn from the English case Dodds v. Walker and accepted in Indian jurisprudence.

Under the corresponding date rule, a period of 'three months' from November 29 expires on February 28 (the corresponding date in the third month following). A notice issued on November 29 with an order date of February 28, 2025 therefore provides exactly three months — and satisfies the statutory requirement. The Court held that there is no basis for reading 'three months' as requiring more than the corresponding calendar month period.

Practical Impact

This ruling clarifies a frequently litigated point in GST proceedings: taxpayers cannot successfully challenge an SCN merely on the ground that it was issued at the tail end of the limitation period, provided the corresponding date gap is met. However, the Court left open the question of whether a notice that gives the taxpayer inadequate practical time to prepare a substantive reply would be treated differently on natural justice grounds — that argument would have to be raised with specific evidence of prejudice.
